events.label: reasoning
events.text: We need answer JSON only. Need assess sequence violation for HVAC-R service call: Thermostats & sequences, observed field condition: no C-wire (power-stealing issues), work in-progress, reference mfr manuals. Need evaluate procedure sequence and state what must happen before work can continue. Need decide pass/fail/needs_more_info. Since no C-wire causing power-stealing issues; likely installing or servicing thermostat that requires C-wire. Sequence violation: should verify power/common wiring and transformer capacity before installing/configuring smart thermostat; if no C-wire, must add common wire or use compatible adapter/transformer per OEM. Work actively in progress; must stop until proper C-wire provided. Decision fail due non-compliant/installed-defective? The component condition categories may apply installed-defective. Risk maybe medium/high? Power-stealing can damage equipment, erratic operation, no immediate safety critical but high? Let's think. The task: "Sequence check: Thermostats & sequences (service call)" "catch any sequence violation". Observed condition no C-wire (power-stealing issues). Work in progress. Need state what must happen before work can continue. So fail because work proceeding without proper C-wire; must install/run C-wire or OEM-approved adapter and verify 24V common before continuing. Component condition: installed-defective (thermostat installation/power supply defective). Risk high? Power stealing can damage controls, compressor short cycling, maybe equipment failure. Could be medium. Let's decide high due potential equipment damage and warranty. Percent complete maybe 40. Value not asked 0. Sound etc default. order maybe empty? The schema includes order for audio/video tasks; not requested, can be empty list. workable maybe empty. findings: "Thermostat is being installed/serviced without a dedicated C-wire; power-stealing operation is causing issues." actions: "Stop work; verify/transformer capacity; install dedicated C-wire or OEM-approved common wire adapter; confirm stable 24 VAC at thermostat before proceeding." references: "OEM service manuals; NEC/NFPA 70 low-voltage wiring; HVAC best practice for 24 VAC common." Need JSON only. Ensure valid JSON.

{
  "decision": "fail",

  "findings": [
    "Thermostat work is in progress without a dedicated C-wire.",
    "Power-stealing operation is causing control instability and can damage the thermostat or HVAC controls.",
    "Sequence violation: proper 24 VAC common source was not verified/established before continuing installation or configuration."
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Stop work immediately.",
    "Verify transformer capacity and 24 VAC availability at the equipment.",
    "Install a dedicated C-wire or use an OEM-approved common-wire adapter/transformer.",
    "Confirm stable 24 VAC between R and C at the thermostat before resuming setup or configuration."
  ],
  "rationale": "A thermostat requiring a C-wire cannot be properly commissioned while relying on power-stealing; the correct sequence is to establish a reliable common power source before mounting, wiring, or configuring the control. Continuing without it risks equipment damage and callbacks.",
  "references": [
    "OEM service manuals",
    "NEC/NFPA 70 low-voltage control wiring requirements",
    "HVAC-R best practice: stable 24 VAC common required for powered thermostats"
  ]
}
